probes.drivingQuestion: How do you turn the comparison between two things that each have a bunch of different qualities (like a song and your taste) into one single number that says how well they match?
probes.ahaRestated: The dot product works like a similarity meter, multiplying matching directions together and adding them up, but because it also scales with length it can be fooled by a "loud" long vector into looking more similar than it really is.
probes.retrievalAnswer: Turn each thing into an arrow (vector), then multiply corresponding entries and add them up, that is the dot product.

map.note: The song/taste framing and the ruler-that-only-checks-one-quality bit made the problem feel concrete and real.
It never says what the numbers actually are, how do you get 'loud' or 'sad' as a number in the first place, so the setup feels a little hand-wavy.
The 'two and one' vs 'one and three' example flew by fast in prose form, I had to reread the sentence to figure out which number multiplied which.
Watching the bars rise and fall as the probe rotates made 'agreement of direction' click for me.
Good twist, but at first I wasn't sure if this was breaking the whole idea or just adding a caveat, took a second to realize this is foreshadowing the crack, not undoing the lesson.
The dot notation 'a . b' shows up with basically no explanation of the symbol itself, just labeled onto the readout; if I hadn't already inferred it from context I'd have been lost.
The doubling demo (4.2 becomes 8.4 exactly) was satisfying and made the linearity claim concrete rather than asserted.
The recap montage and the job-posting aside felt like a victory lap I already understood, attention started to drift here since nothing new was being taught.

advisory.note: Felt clever and satisfying overall, especially the sweep and doubling demos, but a couple of notation moments (a.b, the entry-by-entry recipe) made me feel like I was missing something rather than being taught it.
